does: This paper presents Perceptual Processing Direct Preference Optimization (P²-DPO), a training method that reduces hallucinations in large vision-language models by generating and learning from self-created preference pairs. Rather than relying on human-annotated preferences, the method constructs on-policy preference pairs that specifically target two identified perceptual bottlenecks: focus-and-enhance perception in attended image regions and visual robustness against image degradation. The approach combines a preference pair construction method with calibration mechanisms designed to directly address these visual processing failures, creating a closed-loop system where models learn from their own generated comparisons rather than external annotations.
connects: This work builds on the recent surge in using Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to mitigate hallucinations in vision-language models, extending beyond prior preference-learning approaches that treated vision as a secondary component. It responds to limitations in existing DPO paradigms by addressing the vision-agnostic nature of human preference pairs and their off-policy characteristics. The paper connects to broader research on visual grounding, image degradation robustness, and failure analysis in multimodal systems. It also engages with the growing literature on on-policy learning methods and calibration techniques for improving model reliability, positioning itself as an evolution of DPO that makes visual perception central rather than peripheral to preference learning.
enables: This work opens directions for more perceptually grounded preference optimization in multimodal models, where self-generated data becomes a primary training signal. It suggests future research into identifying and targeting other domain-specific perceptual bottlenecks beyond vision, potentially extending to audio-visual or embodied settings. The on-policy preference construction framework could inspire similar self-correcting paradigms across other modalities. Additionally, the calibration mechanisms developed here may enable more robust vision-language systems under naturalistic conditions with degraded images, potentially leading to better deployment in real-world applications. The approach also positions hallucination reduction as intrinsically tied to improving perceptual processing rather than purely linguistic generation, reframing how the field approaches multimodal reliability.
